description: Comparative 'mo' places the degree of a gradable quality against a spoken or recoverable standard. It forms regular comparisons such as 'mo welao' (better) and 'mo thiku' (smaller). Intensifier 'ru' (INTS) raises degree on its own.
articulatory_notes: Voiced 'm' resonates through the nose behind closed lips. The lips then part into a rounded opening as the tongue draws back for true-mid 'o'; the monosyllable is stressed.
usage_notes: When the standard is stated, its 'sheo' (THAN) phrase precedes the comparative predicate. The fixed sequence 'mo ko' forms the superlative, while 'ru mo' means much more. Context may leave the standard unspoken when it is already clear.

examples.phi: thepalu sheo shelira mo phelora nai.
examples.translation: The garden is more beautiful than the forest.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A comparative reading depends on a standard, whether stated or recoverable. The grammar keeps the relation visible instead of treating better or smaller as self-existing facts.
